name: Housesteads Roman Fort
shortDescription: The best-preserved Roman fort on Hadrian's Wall, with visible barracks and latrines.
description: Vercovicium, as the Romans called it, was built around 124 AD and garrisoned by auxiliary troops from across the empire — Tungrians from modern Belgium and Frisians from the Netherlands. The fort held up to 800 soldiers and its impressive remains include barrack blocks, granaries, a headquarters building, and the famous communal latrines. It sits on the highest point of the Wall with spectacular views in all directions.

name: Chesters Roman Fort
shortDescription: Hadrian's Wall cavalry fort with the finest Roman bathhouse in Britain.
description: Cilurnum was one of the key cavalry forts on Hadrian's Wall, garrisoning a unit of 500 cavalry who could patrol and respond to threats along the frontier. Its bathhouse, preserved to unusual height beside the River North Tyne, is considered the finest Roman military bathhouse in Britain. The site also preserves a remarkable bridge abutment where the Wall crossed the river.

name: Saalburg
shortDescription: A reconstructed Roman fort on the Limes Germanicus, with original artefacts.
description: The Saalburg in the Taunus hills was a cohort fort on the Upper Germanic Limes frontier. Under Kaiser Wilhelm II it was reconstructed in the late 19th century to be 'as Roman as possible,' making it the most completely reconstructed Roman fort north of the Alps. Its museum holds an extraordinary collection of Roman artefacts, and the surrounding Limes earthwork is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Caerleon Roman Fortress
shortDescription: Isca Silurum — one of only three permanent Roman legionary fortresses in Britain.
description: Isca Silurum was founded around 75 AD to control the Silures tribe of Wales and became one of the three permanent legionary bases in Roman Britain. The 2nd Augustan Legion was stationed here for over 200 years. Remarkable remains survive including the only Roman legionary amphitheatre still visible in Britain, stone barrack blocks, and a well-preserved bathhouse.

name: Vindolanda
shortDescription: A pre-Wall Roman fort famous for the Vindolanda Tablets — the oldest handwritten documents in Britain.
description: Vindolanda was an auxiliary fort just south of Hadrian's Wall, pre-dating the Wall itself by about 40 years. The remarkable anaerobic conditions of the waterlogged soil have preserved the Vindolanda Writing Tablets — over 1,000 thin wooden leaves with handwritten messages that represent the oldest surviving handwritten documents in Britain, offering an extraordinary window into life on the Roman frontier.

name: Aquincum
shortDescription: Capital of Roman Pannonia Inferior, on the Danube Limes in modern Budapest.
description: Aquincum was the capital of the Roman province of Pannonia Inferior and is today preserved within Budapest on the west bank of the Danube. It was a major military and civilian settlement with a legionary fortress, amphitheatre, public baths, and aqueducts. The ruins — now an open-air museum — offer insight into how a major Danube Limes city functioned.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage (Danube Limes — tentative list)

name: Dura-Europos
shortDescription: The 'Pompeii of the desert' — a multicultural frontier city frozen in time by a Sassanid siege.
description: Founded by the Seleucids and later occupied by the Parthians and Romans, Dura-Europos was a cosmopolitan city on the Euphrates where Greek, Parthian, Roman, Jewish, and early Christian cultures co-existed. When the Sassanid Persians sacked it around 256 AD, everything was preserved under the sand — including extraordinarily well-preserved frescoes, the oldest known Christian house-church, and evidence of Roman siege warfare.

name: Masada
shortDescription: Herod's desert palace-fortress, site of the last stand against Rome.
description: Built by Herod the Great on a sheer-sided plateau 450 metres above the Dead Sea, Masada served as a desert palace-fortress. In 73 AD, 960 Jewish Zealots held out against 8,000 Roman troops and the Legio X Fretensis for three years. Rather than submit, the defenders chose mass death. The Romans' massive assault ramp — still visible today — is one of the most impressive surviving Roman siege works.
